data.title: Genasys Will Probably Become Profitable In FY26, But Beyond That It Is Still Very Uncertain
data.description:
Genasys Inc. (GNSS) presented its 4Q25 results in mid-December. The results were clearly good and extended the positive trend that began with the ramp-up of the Puerto Rico project, which I covered in my original article on Genasys.

Revenues expanded substantially, by more than 150%, and earnings finally leveraged to positive operating income for the quarter. Management expects this momentum to continue into 2026, potentially leading to full-year profitability for the first time in several years.

The key question, however, is durability. The stock price already embeds a belief that the current business level is sustainable. I am less convinced. Given the valuation already reflects a fairly optimistic scenario beyond the Puerto Rico project, I maintain a Hold rating.

4Q25 results

Genasys reported a very strong fourth quarter.

Revenues reached about $17 million, up more than 150% YoY and more than 70% sequentially, marking the largest revenue quarter in the company’s history. This growth was sufficient to push operating income into positive territory.

That said, the composition of revenue and growth matters because it affects durability (more on this later). The overwhelming majority of the growth came from the Puerto Rico emergency warning system project. Of the $17 million reported in the quarter, around $7.5 million was attributable to PR, with another meaningful contribution from a single US Navy hardware order. Without Puerto Rico, growth is still very positive (almost 50% at $10 million versus $6.7 million last year), but more modest, and more important, not enough to leverage the company into profits.

For the full year, Genasys generated about $41 million in revenues, up roughly 70% YoY. Of this, $13.2 million came from Puerto Rico. Excluding PR, revenues still grew at a decent 16% rate, driven mainly by small hardware contracts, typically in the $1–2 million range, largely related to LRAD deployments across nuclear security, wildlife protection, and other niche use cases.

This distinction is important. Puerto Rico is a large, multi-year project with a scale far above Genasys’ historical contract size. The rest of the business, while growing, remains fragmented and project-based, without clear evidence yet of another PR-scale opportunity in execution.

When we move to profitability, the revenue growth delivered real operating leverage. Gross margins in the quarter exceeded 50%, helped by a favorable hardware mix, and management indicated that this level is sustainable going forward. More importantly, operating expenses fell YoY. SG&A declined by more than 25%, reflecting lower marketing spend, lower professional services costs, tax credits, and tighter labor and R&D discipline.

As a result, operating income reached approximately $1.3 million in 4Q25, compared to a large loss in the prior year. Adjusted EBITDA was about $2.4 million.

Forward: breakeven point and durability

Management stated on the call that Genasys expects to become profitable in FY26, including at the net income level. Based on the 4Q25 results, this appears credible.

Using the quarter as a reference, the operating breakeven threshold seems to be around $15–17 million in quarterly revenues, or roughly $60 million annually. At that level, the company can generate HSD operating margins, assuming gross margins remain near 50% and opex discipline holds.

The backlog supports this view, at least in the near term. Genasys exited the year with approximately $60 million in backlog, largely related to Puerto Rico. Of the $75 million total PR contract, only $13 million has been recognized so far, leaving more than $60 million to be executed over the coming years, mostly in 2026.

In FY25, the company generated roughly $27 million in revenues outside of PR. If Genasys recognizes about half of the remaining PR backlog next year, it can reach or exceed the $60 million revenue threshold required for profitability. In other words, profitability in FY26 is not a stretch.

The problem lies beyond that horizon. Management mentioned receiving increased inbound interest from large actors (state governments, agencies) for Puerto Rico-like solutions. While encouraging, these remain inquiries, not signed contracts. Until another large, multi-year deployment is announced, Genasys remains heavily dependent on a single project. In that sense, the company is still a one-trick pony.

Cash and burn rate

Cash is a different discussion, with more potential risks. The company has $8 million in cash and $18 million in debt. That debt is due in May 2026, and management said on the call that it expects to be able to repay it from cash flows: "We expect cash flow receipts and disbursements to support paying off the entire debt on time."

However, when we observe the quarterly cash flows for FY25, we observe that working capital has been a user of cash, implying the PR or other contracts are billed but not yet paid by the customers. This was particularly true in 4Q25, with $6 million used in cash to fund receivables ($3.5 million net of inventories consumed). Indeed, the company had to extend the loan from ~$14 million to $18 million in 2Q25 because it needed the cash to build working capital.

Potentially, management knows/expects that some of the ~$14 million in receivables will be paid during 1H26 (fiscal), or some of the $15 million in inventories converted to cash, helping build the cushion needed to repay the debt.

In case that didn't happen, the company does not list a credit facility or another source of liquidity on its 10-K under Liquidity. However, given the security provided by the PR and other contracts, I do not think it would have significant trouble finding at least a bridge loan or extension for a year to cover the May 2026 debt, if it didn't get the funds from operating cash flows.

That said, if for some reason the company faces operating challenges in late 2025 or early 2026, and the receipt of receivables is questioned, or future profitability or contract completion is questioned, the timing of the debt payment adds a risk, which should be taken into account.

Valuation already prices in the next trick

At current prices, GNSS trades at a market cap of around $100 million.

Assuming $60 million in revenues next year, with operating income of about $6 million (roughly in line with the $1.3–1.5 million generated in 4Q25, annualized), after approximately $1.5 million in interest expense and a normalized 25% tax rate, net income would be around $3.5 million.

That implies a forward P/E of close to 30x, for a year that recognized half of PR's backlog. If the project is developed faster, say 80% of the remaining revenue is recognized, and the company reaches $75/80 million in revenues, then operating income could reach $10 million ($39 million gross profits at 50%, and OpEx flat at $30 million), and net profits of $6 million. Still a 15x PE.

This valuation only makes sense if Puerto Rico is not the peak, but the beginning of a larger phase, with new projects replacing PR as it winds down and allowing revenues to remain consistently above $60–70 million.

In effect, the stock price already embeds the announcement of another large-scale deployment, potentially even larger than Puerto Rico; otherwise, the company should be worth much less, because the ex-PR business cannot generate profitability.

I do not believe those expectations are conservative. If Puerto Rico ends without a comparable substitute, the company likely reverts to lower revenues and renewed losses. The asymmetry is clear: execution risk is limited in the near term, but strategic risk remains high over the medium term.

For that reason, despite acknowledging the operational turnaround and the likelihood of near-term profitability, I believe GNSS is not an opportunity. The market is paying today for growth that has not yet been secured. Accordingly, I maintain a Hold rating.

data.title: BlackBerry: A Question Of Leadership Vision And Investor Value
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome KTW Advisory as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

BlackBerry (BB), the once popular phone company, has been undergoing a decade-long transition into a software-first company. This transition has not been easy and has been fraught with failures and mismanagement. However, there's a compelling story underneath if leadership can button down a strategic vision to unlock potential. In this article, I will highlight both risks and opportunities to this potential and why I am cautiously optimistic that BlackBerry can finally provide upside value over the next 2-3 years.

Preservation Vs. Vision

Charles Dickens's novel A Tale of Two Cities is famous for its opening line, "It was the best of times, it was the worst of times." I know it is a cliché, but it is a fitting one to describe how investors feel after BlackBerry's Q3 2026 results highlighted a company that is straddling the line between significant opportunity and discouraging mediocrity. This present state was born from a decade of mismanagement and a recent questionable leadership decision BlackBerry's board made in 2021.

A few years prior, in February 2019, BB had acquired Cylance for $1.4 billion, which was meant to be the crown jewel in BB's cybersecurity aspirations. In Oct 2021, it brought on its current CEO, John Giamatteo, to lead the cybersecurity business. At the time, the cybersecurity segment was already facing headwinds as revenues declined from $532 million in FY2020 to $491 million in FY2021. Over the next few years under Giamatteo's guidance, BB's cybersecurity division declined ever further while losing significant market share to competitors like CrowdStrike and SentinelOne.

In parallel, Mattias Eriksson was brought on to lead the IoT division and successfully steered the Software-Defined Vehicle (SDV) strategy and oversaw significant QNX growth. However, when the Board decided to look for a new CEO in Nov 2023, the Board interestingly chose Giamatteo. While Giamatteo is a competent operational CEO, the move signaled a preference for safe preservation over the visionary leadership Eriksson "potentially" represented. The board's move lacked the type of "two-steps ahead" strategic vision and moxie that investors require to differentiate BB from its past decade of underperformance.

This lack of leadership vision is heard on the Q3 2026 earnings call. For example, when Canaccord's analyst asked specifically about the China EV win, Giamatteo mentioned it was more about "cost savings" and "BOM optimization." Later, Robert Baird's analyst attempted a similar question. I personally would have liked to hear Giamatteo answer those questions in a way that promotes BB's superior product and distinct value proposition, particularly as BB is releasing additional solutions further up the price ramp. It's these finer details that separate the visionaries from the safe incumbents.

Financial Identity Crisis

BlackBerry's current identity is muddled. The question for investors is whether BB represents a high-growth tech play or a legacy value trap?

Stagnant Growth: Looking at the financials between FY25 and FY26 (note: FY26 is completed using Q4 figures), total revenue is "only" growing at 1% year-over-year. This nullifies BB's growth story for any investor who screens for "high growth" companies.

The Growth Gap: Investors who are more familiar with BB will understand the breakdown of the segments and will point out QNX, which is expected to grow 13% year-over-year. However, I personally would like to see QNX maintain a >15% growth rate, which would be typical of high-tech success stories.

Cash Without Yield: The company is "flush with cash" (~$378M total cash and investments as of Q3 FY26), yet offers no dividends nor has hinted at any accretive deployment of this excess cash. To note, there is an additional cash expectation of $38M in Feb 2026 (from Arctic Wolf) and another $30M in May 2026 (from Malikie).

P/S Comps: Currently, BlackBerry is sitting at a P/S of ~4.1, while the industry median is close to ~3.3. The key valuation metrics show that BB is likely priced correctly with very limited value upside.

In a market that has seen roughly 79% gains over the last year, BlackBerry remains down ~8% over the same period. BB's leadership has failed to sell the BB story to investors.

QNX: The Rocket Ship to Nowhere

During Canaccord's growth conference in Aug 2025, Giamatteo described QNX as a "rocket ship" for growth; however, investors are now being faced with signs of slower than anticipated growth and potential missteps.

At the 2022 AGM (under ex-CEO John Chen), BB presented a deck that highlighted projected future IoT revenue of $307 million for FY25. Currently, the FY26 expectations sit at $266 million-meaning the company is nearly 13% behind its own three-year-old targets even with the additional year! This is a massive enough miss that it calls into question the strategy.

Management cites "ecosystem delays" but insists on a "stable moat" with the upcoming SDV 8.0. While the 20-million-vehicle increase in QNX deployments is a stable floor, it lacks the explosive growth investors were led to expect. Is this moat an actual moat if Giamatteo and BB cannot apply prices that can actually grow the company's revenues?

Canaccord attempted to address the vision on the Q3 2026 earnings call, and John seemed more focused on doubling down on investing in GTM and sales activities. This seems like the same failed strategy that befell Cylance. In fact, Chen credited Giamatteo for a similar strategic shift during his Cylance tenure to leverage more marketing, recruit sales talent, and aggressive GTM strategies. It does seem like you cannot teach an old dog new tricks; ramping up sales and GTM is not strategic nor is it visionary-it's more of the same.

Secure Comms the new Cylance?

Beyond IoT, BlackBerry retains a secure communications business (largely government contracts). However, this segment is currently a distraction:

  1. Revenue is shrinking at an 8% YoY rate.
  2. While the IoT segment alone shows ~10-13% growth, when bundled with the declining Secure Comms and lumpy Licensing revenue (contracting at -2%), total software revenue growth is dragged down to a negligible 1.2%.
  3. SecureComms did achieve various certifications (BSI, FedRAMP high); however, it didn't seem to translate to accretive growth, which is blamed on "a stronger implementation cycle in 2025." However, the DBNRR is recorded at 92% for the quarter, which highlights other underlying issues.

Within Secure Comms, there are pockets of sub-segments that are doing well, but I question whether this management team can grow this book of business or if they are falling for the same mistakes that eroded the Cylance business.

The Hidden Upside: Malikie and Arctic Wolf

For value seekers, two interesting pieces remain on the board: the Malikie Patent Deal and the Arctic Wolf Equity.

The Malikie Patent Deal: BB sold 30,000+ patents to Malikie for $200m, which consisted of a $170 million upfront payment and a $30 million payment due by May 2026. With active lawsuits against Samsung, Nintendo, Canon, Marathon, Core Scientific, and several other digital mining companies and recent settlements with D-Link, Intel, and Huawei, the profit-sharing agreement (ranging from 8% to 50%) could provide a non-dilutive cash windfall once Malikie is made whole. (It should be noted that Malikie purports that some of the technology underpinning decentralized Bitcoin infringes on ECC technology patented by BlackBerry, which could be an interesting prospect.)

The Arctic Wolf Equity: Arctic Wolf purchased the Cylance unit for $160M and still owes BB ~$38M (due around Feb 2026). Critically, BlackBerry holds 5.5 million shares in Arctic Wolf. With a private market "fair value" of roughly $44M ($8/share), this is a sweetener for new investors. But for long-term holders, there exists the painful reminder that a once $1.4B asset was eroded down to $200M.

Capital Tug-of-War

BlackBerry's cash position ($378M) is anchored by a $200M, 3% convertible senior note, which is due by 2029. This note is a continuation of the debentures long held by Prem Watsa's Fairfax, which has historically caused angst due to its dilutive nature once the stock hits the ~$3.88-$5.00 conversion range. The note can be converted to ~51.5M new common shares, which would represent about 8% of existing outstanding shares. While 3% interest is absolutely great terms, the argument could be made that BB didn't require such a large amount, could have used other structures (credit lines), and inevitably harmed investors with these notes. The redemption rights for BB trigger on Feb 22, 2027, and currently, BB has enough cash to settle the $200M; however, if BB stock trades above $5.04 for consecutive days, there's a high likelihood the holders will convert their notes. That creates a logical ceiling for share price and will be a price of interest if BB ever trades up to that point again.

The company's share buyback program also appears to be some smoke and mirrors, as the buybacks mostly offset the significant amount of share-based compensation leadership is given. While this helps shield investors from BB's own internal dilution from compensation, it doesn't actually increase shareholder value.

Valuation

I modeled various scenarios based on leadership being able to grow the business using basic DCF and EBITDA multiples. I created forecasts based on QNX growth of 15-20%, which encompasses my belief that QNX 8.0 will increase QNX prices by almost double over time. I believe a growth rate of 8% is achievable. I used an 8% discount rate and extrapolated FCF for Q3 to arrive at a $72.4M starting cash flow. The 8% discount rate was derived using a CAPM model, where I used a beta of 1.01, risk premium of 4.6%, and current risk free rates. I also took into account the amount of net cash BB has, which included the Arctic Wolf and Malikie mandatory payments in cash and cash equivalents. Based on these assumptions, I believe the fair value of shares would be $4.27.

Reviewing the SA Peers for P/S comps, one could say that BB is fairly valued at 4.3x (a premium to the peer average of 3.3x). But, if you believe in the BB growth story and the moat mechanics, other comps such as Cadence Design System, Inc. (16.6x) and Synopsys (11.1x) could be more relevant. Both companies are operating in infrastructure software, and both have the "moat" mechanics I believe allow for an approximate comparison. Those comparisons could reflect the upside potential valuation of BB.

Conclusion: Is There Value Here?

So, is the value there for BB? Personally, I am cautiously optimistic due to the recent price dip below $4, but there are several unanswered questions about leadership's vision and execution. Investors are placing a huge bet on BB's leadership executing on several aforementioned fronts. I commend Giamatteo for stopping a sinking ship, though navigating to the next destination requires a bold vision, and investors are tired of treading water.

data.title: Medical Properties Trust: Lumpy Recovery & Rich Dividend Yields Still Compelling - Risks Remain
data.description:
I previously covered Medical Properties Trust, Inc. (MPW) in September 2025, discussing the REIT's promising FQ2'25 performance and the potential outperformance against the prior target of "annualized cash rent of more than $1 billion by year-end 2026."

Given that its tenant default headwinds might already be behind us, with the expensive debt refinancing allowing them to sustain their ongoing restructuring process and the discounted valuations contributing to the rich dividend investment thesis, I had reiterated my Buy rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the MPW stock, thanks to the sequentially stable performance metrics, the management's earlier than expected dividend payout raise/new share repurchase program, and the dual pronged return prospects across capital appreciation/dividend incomes.

This is despite the likely to be lumpy recovery process arising from the industry-wide healthcare headwinds, the impacted balance sheet health, and the potential volatility arising from the elevated short interest ratio.

MPW's Performance Metrics Showing Signs Of Recovery

MPW 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy rating, MPW has outperformed with a single digit total gain at +7.7% against the wider market at +3.4%, thanks to the recent AI-related meltdown arising from valuations/debt risks already triggering a notable market rotation to deep-value plays, such as MPW.

This is significantly aided by the earlier than expected dividend raise by +12.5% to $0.09 per share in November 2025, with it effectively putting an end to the frozen dividends at $0.08 per share since Q3'24 - attributed to the prior financial covenant surrounding the Steward re-tenanting process ending Q3'25.

The payout raise naturally highlights MPW management's confidence about delivering both a healthier tenant profile in 2026 and the "pro rata annualized cash rent from our current portfolio will exceed $1 billion by the end of 2026," as similarly observed in the unexpected share repurchase authorization at $150M.

The same has been observed in the FQ3'25 rental revenues of $217.4M across rent billed/straight-line rent (inline QoQ/+5.3% YoY) and the growing Adjusted Annualized EBITDAre at $906.28M (inline QoQ/+3.3% YoY) - with it signaling the much needed stabilization on a QoQ basis/improvement in its performance metrics on a YoY basis.

At the same time, with the Steward/Prospect tenant defaults behind us, MPW appears well positioned to report sequential recoveries in FY2026 as well, aided by the improved margin of safety arising from Circle Health, the largest tenant comprising 22.8% of its FQ3'25 revenues, turning profitable in 2024 after the trough 2023 performance.

Across its other tenants, the REIT has also reported an expanding Total Potfolio TTM EBITDAre Rent Coverage at 2.5x in FQ3'25 (inline QoQ/+0.2x YoY), with the "collected materially all cash rent owed by these tenants through October" 2025, aside from some smaller exceptions, underscoring the resilience of its current tenant base after numerous tenant defaults.

MPW's Future Recovery Likely To Be Lumpy

On the other hand, I am of the opinion that MPW's recovery is unlikely to be linear/smooth sailing, with the hefty interest expenses of $132.39M (+2% QoQ/+24.6% YoY) already triggering the deteriorating Normalized Funds from Operations [NFFO] of $0.13 per share (-7.1% QoQ/-18.7% YoY).

The higher interest expense poses risks to the REIT's future recovery indeed, given the extremely inflated Adjusted Net Debt to Transaction Adjusted Annualized EBITDA re Ratio of 9.6x in FQ3'25 and the deteriorating Adjusted Interest Coverage Ratio of 1.8x, compared to a quarter ago at 9.6x/1.8x, a year ago at 9.5x/2.1x, and 2019 levels at 6x/5.17x, respectively.

This is on top of the $82M in net impairments arising from Prospect/Pennsylvania/Rhode Island assets, with it remaining to be seen when the Yale/Connecticut cash settlements may be finalized to "repay MPT's outstanding DIP loan balances" - with these presenting near-term headwinds to MPW's bottom-line performance.

These reasons are also why I urge readers to temper their expectations surrounding the REIT's future recovery, pending the tailwinds from numerous legal resolutions and the consequently improved cash flow conversion.

This is especially since MPW is unlikely to embark on any aggressive asset acquisitions despite the supposedly cheaper borrowing cost environment arising from the Fed's ongoing rate cut, attributed to the ongoing headwinds faced by numerous healthcare operators.

The headwinds may persist through 2026 as observed in the ongoing bankruptcies observed in 2024/2025, attributed to "rising costs, workforce shortages and reimbursement challenges," along with "a shift in patient volumes and regulatory changes."

The same has been observed in the insurance sectors as well, with it apparent that MPW faces mounting recovery/growth headwinds in the intermediate term, with these metrics exemplifying why the stock is only suitable for those with a long-term investing trajectory and higher risk tolerance.

MPW's Ongoing Recovery Still Attractively Valued

The Consensus Forward Estimates

As a result of the raised dividend payouts and the positive implication on MPW's future bottom-line performance, it is unsurprising that the consensus already expects a sustained FFO per share expansion beyond LTM levels of $0.59 to FY2026 estimates of $0.65 and up to FY2030 estimates of $0.83, with it implying an excellent bottom-line growth at a 5Y CAGR of +7%.

MPW Valuations

For now, MPW may appear somewhat inflated at FWD Price/FFO valuations of 9.33x, thanks the ongoing upgrades from the 2.31x observed in January 2024, compared to the 5Y mean of 7.80x and the 2019 pre-pandemic mean of 12.72x.

On the other hand, given the REIT's ongoing turnaround process and completion only expected by the end of 2026, I believe that it may be more prudent to refer to the consensus FY2027 FFO per share estimates of $0.70 as a normalized metric.

Assuming so, based on the current stock prices of $5.14, MPW appears relatively cheap at my estimated FY2027 Price/FFO valuations of 7.34x, with it apparent that MPW remains compelling upon the normalization in its performance metrics after the challenging FY2023/FY2024/FY2025 and the transition FY2026.

A similar conclusion may also be observed after comparing to the diversified healthcare REIT peers, including:

  • CareTrust REIT, Inc. (CTRE) at FWD Price/FFO valuations of 20.59x and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 13.45x,
  • Community Healthcare Trust Incorporated (CHCT) at 7.39x/16.04x,
  • Global Medical REIT Inc. (GMRE) at 8.38x/13.54x,
  • Healthcare Realty Trust Incorporated (HR) at 10.52x/18.54x, respectively,

with it apparent that MPW is still offering an excellent value Buy opportunity for contrarian investors.

MPW Remains A Great Buy

MPW 20Y Stock Price

For now, MPW's bottom has emerged by January 2024 as the stock consistently chart higher lows over the past few quarters, with it exemplifying the growing bullish support surrounding its upcoming rebound after nearly 8 quarters of sideways trading.

MPW Technical Indicators

The same may also be observed in MPW's still reasonable technical indicators across the RSIs and trading volumes, with the bulls consistently supporting its upward trend and potential breakout from current levels of $5.10s beyond the prior resistance levels of $5.60s/$6.40s.

Based on the consensus FY2027 FFO per share estimates of $0.70 and the 2019 pre-pandemic mean of 12.72x, there remains an excellent upside potential of +75.5% to my long-term price target of $8.90 as well, upon the normalization in its future tenants health and consequently, the improvement in its performance metrics.

Most importantly, readers must note that MPW's NFFO Payout ratio remains low at 69.2% in FQ3'25, compared to the sector median of 74.12%, with it implying a potential for further hike moving forward.

Assuming a similar payout ratio, readers may look forward to a sustained payout raise from current annualized levels of $0.36 to 2030 levels of $0.61 as well (based on the consensus FY2030 estimates of $0.83), expanding at a 5Y CAGR of +11.1%.

As a result of the dual pronged return prospects across capital appreciation and dividend income at current yields of 7%, I believe that MPW remains a compelling Buy for dividend/value-oriented investors, with the next few years likely to bring forth a much needed stabilization in its performance metrics along with the slow but steady deleveraging path.

The latter is also why the stock is only suitable for contrarian investors, worsened by the potential volatility arising from the still elevated short interest ratio of 25.4% - with it implying the stock's likely to be lumpy price trajectory in the mean time.

data.title: PAPI: Attractive Monthly Distribution, Still Waiting For The Right Environment
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The Parametric Equity Premium Income ETF (PAPI) has provided relatively muted returns since our prior update, though slightly positive, so it wasn't all bad news. This exchange-traded fund utilizes a call writing strategy on a portfolio that is pretty close to equal-weight across all sectors, except underweighted in communication services.

With that, seeing it lag against the S&P 500 Index isn't all that unexpected. That will occur in most time periods for this fund, barring a period where the technology sector starts to underperform or moves mostly sideways. As the S&P 500 Index has been fairly stretched in terms of valuation on the back of primarily the Magnificent 7, that isn't necessarily something that is off the cards.

On the other hand, the Mag 7 names continue to primarily provide the earnings growth that helps to power their share prices higher as well. One of the problems with stretched valuations is that they need to keep meeting such lofty expectations.

PAPI Basics

  • Dividend Frequency: Monthly
  • Dividend Yield: 2.91% SEC yield, 7.45% TTM Distribution Yield
  • Expense Ratio: 0.29%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $269.77 million
  • Structure: Active ETF

PAPI's investment objective is "to provide consistent monthly income while maintaining prospects for capital appreciation." They do this by investing in a "diversified portfolio of high dividend stocks, selected based on yield and risk metrics." They then "generate incremental income from proprietary S&P 500 (SPY) call overwriting strategy." This could also include selling calls on the S&P 500 Index itself, as well as SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust. The portfolio will be primarily companies selected from the Russell 3000 Index.

The Fund will systemically sell (write) out-of-the-money call option contracts, which have an expiration date of approximately two weeks, with an objective of generating incremental income. The Fund will sell such call option contracts on the Underlying ETF or on the Underlying Index. Flexible Exchange Options (“FLEX Options”) that reference the Underlying ETF may be utilized. The Fund’s derivative instruments are generally limited to its call option writing strategy.

Performance Comparison

I've stated in previous write-ups on PAPI that I believed Parametric (Eaton Vance owned by Morgan Stanley (MS)) missed an opportunity to provide a similar strategy that they operate in several closed-end funds. That could be Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Opportunities Fund (ETV), Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund (ETB) or Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Diversified Equity Income Fund (ETY).

These funds all operate similar option writing strategies against the S&P 500 Index, with slightly different weightings and index approach, like ETV, which incorporates the Nasdaq as well.

As we can see, those CEF cousin funds have all easily outperformed PAPI. Under most scenarios, this is set to continue as well. ETY is the best of the group so far, historically, since PAPI's performance is thanks to ETY's partial overwrite strategy of targeting around 50%. ETV and ETB both target nearly the entire portfolio being overwritten and thus, have run into the upside cap that is common for call writing funds during bull markets.

If they had constructed it similarly to ETY, it could have been a strong competitor against other ETF peers such as the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX) or NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI). The benefit of the ETF structure over a closed-end fund structure is that some investors don't like dealing with the discounts/premiums that can occur (I view that as a key opportunity, but others get frustrated with them).

With that, instead, PAPI gets smoked by both of those peers.

Why PAPI Underperforms

Most investors may be familiar with this by now, but basically, if the S&P 500 Index is trading at $95 and they write calls at $100 to collect a $2 premium, the maximum gain is $102. So if the index rises to $105, that would mean the portion overwritten would underperform. This is a super simple example, but it is imperative to understanding why we see PAPI underperforming so significantly.

This is because PAPI takes more of an equal-weighted approach to sectors.

With these weightings, the top ten holdings come out to some unusual names that you don't see in a lot of other portfolios.

In the case of the other funds, they take more of what would be considered a covered call approach — even if it is indirectly. The other funds also write index options within their strategies, but the underlying portfolios are weighted toward the actual S&P 500 Index holdings because they hold similar weightings in their portfolio to the index itself.

Here is a look at ETY:

GPIX:

Then NEOS doesn't appear to provide a sector breakdown on their site, but Seeking Alpha reflects these weightings, and with the given top holdings, I would suspect it to be highly accurate:

This becomes important because going back to our simple example above. The S&P 500 Index goes from $95 to $105 — these other funds' underlying portfolios will appreciate by that or very similarly to that $10 in appreciation.

For PAPI, the underlying portfolio mismatch to their SPX short call options creates a situation where they can participate in losses on their options strategy while also not having that offset by the underlying portfolio. That creates a double-whammy for the fund.

To confirm, here were PAPI's latest short call option positions at the time of writing:

SECURITY DESCRIPTION SECURITY TYPE METHOD SHARES/PAR MARKET VALUE (USD)
4SPY CALL USD 12/22/2025 OPTIONS Physical -1,309 -131.25
4SPY CALL USD 12/26/2025 OPTIONS Physical -1,310 -14,805.71
4SPY CALL USD 12/31/2025 OPTIONS Physical -1,330 -403,231.80

They've incorporated a laddered approach, which is a positive, in my opinion. However, that hasn't been too helpful relative to the underlying portfolio to overwrite mismatch strategy they've constructed.

While no one knows how long tech will continue to outperform, we have clearly seen that being the case for the last few years now. To confirm, we can compare the broader equity index tracking ETF, such as the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), against the Invesco Equal-Weighted S&P 500 ETF (RSP). It may seem shocking, but at one point, SPY didn't absolutely stomp RSP — a period where there was a closer relationship between the two before the Mag 7 names became such a dominant force.

This all does bring up the point that PAPI may not be a terrible investment going forward, but one that has underperformed and will be set to continue to underperform other call writing peers should tech continue to dominate. The added diversification of PAPI can be a benefit to some investors who are looking for that, which I actually incorporate myself in the form of value-oriented CEFs or even the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) as a rather sizeable holding for myself.

Waiting for diversification to potentially pay off in the long run, the fund also delivers a fairly attractive distribution yield — while it varies from month to month like most ETFs, it has still been relatively stable. The trailing twelve-month yield comes in at a respectable 7.45% and the shares have appreciated ~6% since their launch.

Conclusion

PAPI utilizes an equal-weighted sector allocation, providing diversification relative to the broader equity indices. They then apply an overwrite strategy against that basket of equity positions via short S&P 500 Index call options. That brings in a premium that is then paid out as an attractive monthly distribution.

However, with this approach, the fund is positioned with a mismatch portfolio relative to the call writing strategy. That creates situations not only where the upside is capped due to the overwrite, but where the portfolio may not perform to help compensate for some of that upside cap. With that, we see the result we've expected to see since the fund's inception, which is lackluster returns relative to its other call writing peers.

It isn't all bad news, though. Should the Mag 7 names start to underperform and equal-weighted portfolios start to work relatively better as they have historically done previously, that could set up PAPI to be a winner. That makes it appropriate for a fairly unique situation, which we just haven't seen for the last several years now.

data.title: Bristol-Myers Squibb: Contrarian Buy For Dividend Hunters, Despite Mounting Growth Headwinds
data.description:
I previously covered Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY) in August 2025, discussing how their double-beat FQ2'25 performance and raised FY2025 guidance had been met with much skepticism, as observed in the steep pullback post earnings.

Given the robust Growth Portfolio performance, the intensified R&D/partnerships/M&A efforts, the discounted valuations presenting a deep-value opportunity, and the rich cash flow generation, I had reiterated my buy rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the BMY stock despite the outsized rally/overbought technical indicators and the growth headwinds arising from the patent cliffs/Medicaid deals with the US government.

With the pharmaceutical giant still boasting rich cash flows, healthier balance sheet, and secure/rich dividend investment thesis, I am of the opinion that contrarian investors may still add the stock upon a moderate retracement.

BMY Is A Beneficiary Of The Ongoing Market Rotation

BMY 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy rating, BMY has indeed bottomed at the $42s floor by October 2025, before bouncing off those levels and rapidly charting an outsized double digits rally over the past few weeks.

The latter is unsurprising indeed, with the recent AI-related meltdown arising from valuations/debt risks already triggering a notable market rotation to deep-value plays, such as BMY, and the great upward momentum also lending credence to my two Buy ratings over the past few months.

Much of the tailwinds are likely attributed to BMY's consistently raised FY2025 guidance over the past three quarters before arriving at the latest revenue outlook at $47.75B at the midpoint (-1.1% YoY) and adj EPS at $6.50 (+465.2% YoY).

Despite the lumpy YTD revenue performance at $35.69B (-0.7% YoY) and adj EPS at $4.89 (+1,022.6% YoY), it is apparent that the ongoing FY2025 guidance upgrades have highlighted its promising portfolio renewal efforts.

The same has been observed in BMY's Growth Portfolio already comprising 56.5% of its overall top-lines in FQ3'25 (+7.8 points YoY), with the accelerating Growth Portfolio at +17% YoY already well balancing the Legacy Portfolio's deterioration at -13% YoY despite the tougher comparison from a year ago at +20% YoY and +1% YoY, respectively.

These developments lend credibility to its consistently raised top-line guidance, as discussed above, as the management executes against the patent cliff headwinds through the robust in-house/acquired pipeline performance.

Risks To BMY's Investment Thesis

On the other hand, I am of the opinion that the rally may have occurred rather fast and furious, since BMY's Opdivo will face patent cliff headwinds from 2028 onwards - a valid concern since the therapy is the lead driver at FQ3'25 Growth Portfolio revenues of $2.53B (+6% YoY) or the equivalent 20.7% of its overall revenues.

While other therapies such as Reblozyl, Opdualag, Breyanzi, Camzyos, and Krazati may have boasted high double digit revenue growth on a YoY basis aided by the delayed patent expiry between 2031-2037, these merely comprise 13.2% of the overall revenues - with it apparent that BMY is likely to face further top/bottom-line headwinds over the next few years.

At the same time, readers must note that BMY has offered Eliquis "for Free to Medicaid Program" starting on January 1, 2026, with it likely to trigger headwinds to its FY2026 performance, since the therapy comprises 30.6% of its overall FQ3'25 revenues.

While this deal has been structured to trigger a 3Y tariff relief, an important takeaway since 68% of BMY's FQ3'25 revenues are based in the US worsened by the "significant manufacturing operations in the U.S., Puerto Rico, Switzerland, Ireland, and the Netherlands," I am of the opinion that the concession may have been overly done.

Perhaps, part of the decision may be attributed to the fact that Eliquis will face patent expiry headwinds from 2028 onwards, with the management seemingly accelerating the inevitable earlier by two years.

This may perhaps explain why the therapy has been relegated under the Legacy Portfolio since the detailed breakout in FQ1'22 despite being a top-line drive at FQ3'25 revenues of $3.74B (+25% YoY).

This is especially since Eliquis' alternative therapies, including Aspirin and Pradaxa, are higher rated than the former while likely to be cheaper attributed to the latter two's generic availability since 1930 and 2022, respectively, with the former likely to face price erosion from 2028 onwards, if not for the Eliquis deal with the US government.

Otherwise, while FY2027 is likely to bring forth a normalized YoY comparison, I am of the opinion that BMY may report a mixed performance in FY2026, with the outsized growth profile observed in the Growth Portfolio unlikely to balance the patent cliff headwinds.

This is on top of the Medicaid deal triggering the upcoming launch of new medicines "with a more balanced pricing approach across developed nations" and the direct access for cash paying patients for Sotyktu® (deucravacitinib), Zeposia® (ozanimod), Reyataz® (atazanavir), Baraclude® (entecavir), and Zeposia ® SC (abatacept) at hefty "discounts approximately 80% off current list prices," since Sotyktu, Zeposia, and Zeposia comprise $1.2B or the equivalent ~9.8% of its FQ3'25 revenues.

BMY's Rich Cash Flows Have Been Overlooked

For now, the silver lining to BMY's investment thesis will be its robust cash flow provided by YTD operating activities at $12.18B (+13.3% YoY) and the consequently accelerated deleveraging cadence as observed in:

  • the lower net debts of $32.06B (+22.4% YoY),
  • the somewhat stable net-debt-to-annualized-FQ3'25-adj-EBITDA ratio of 1.40x (compared to 1.41x in FQ3'24), and
  • the still decent adj-EBITDA-to-interest-expenses ratio of 2.96x (compared to 4.10x in FQ3'24).

This is based on the FQ3'25 adj EBITDA at approximately $5.7B (-31.3% YoY) arising from the adj operating incomes of $4.69B (inline YoY) and the depreciation and amortization at $1.01B (-61.1% YoY).

While FY2026 is likely to bring forth an impacted cash flow generation arising from the Medicaid deals with the US government, I am of the opinion that the healthier balance sheet and the still secure dividend payouts (to be discussed in the segment) have been rather impressive, with it perhaps explaining why the stock has been able to outperform since the October 2025 bottom.

BMY Valuations

Despite the +27.2% rally, readers must note that BMY remains discounted at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 8.29x as well, near to its 1Y mean of 7.74x albeit down notably from the sector median of 19.50x, the 5Y mean of 18.40x, and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 21.80x.

If anything, similarly discounted valuations have been observed in:

  • Pfizer Inc. (PFE) at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 8.08x, compared to the 5Y mean of 10.38x and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 13.97x,
  • Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) at 11.27x/17.37x/19.21x, and
  • Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) (NONOF) at 13.31x/30.14x/20.91x, respectively,

with it apparent that BMY's oversold position is not unique, since it also affects other pharmaceutical peers facing patent cliff/execution risks in varying degrees.

This reason is also why the stock is only suitable for those with a longer investing trajectory, since BMY's intermediate term top/bottom-line performance and discounted valuations are likely to underperform expectations.

This is before the eventual accretion of its new in-house/acquired therapies on the Growth Portfolio and the consequent boost on its top/bottom-lines/valuations.

BMY Remains A Great Buy

BMY 5Y Stock Price

For now, BMY has broken out of numerous resistance levels and the 50/100/200 day moving averages to retest the next resistance levels of $54s, with the bullish upward momentum seemingly built upon several quarters of consecutive double beat performances and the outsized Growth Portfolio performance.

Based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 8.29x and the management's FY2025 adj EPS guidance of $6.50 at the midpoint, it is apparent that the stock is still trading near to my fair value estimates of $53.80.

Based on the mixed adj EPS prospects over the next few years of patent cliff/IPRD charges, I am of the opinion that BMY is likely to continue trading sideways at current levels, pending the materialization of renewed growth opportunities beyond the current patent cliff headwinds.

On the other hand, I am of the opinion that BMY's discounted valuations and the mixed performance metrics have triggered great opportunities for those looking to buy deep-value, dividend stocks.

The same has been observed in the still rich forward dividend yields of 4.65% by the time of writing - expanded against the 5Y mean of 3.23% despite the moderation from October 2025 heights of 5.82% attributed to the recent rally.

Thanks to the richer YTD net cash provided by/(used in) operating activities at $12.18B (+13.3% YoY), it is apparent that BMY's YTD dividend obligations at $3.78B (+3.8% YoY) remain extremely well covered, albeit somewhat negated by the underwhelming payout hike of +1.6% in December 2025 against the 5Y averages of +6.62%.

As a result of the robust dual pronged return prospects across the secure dividend income and the speculative capital appreciation upon upward re-rating along with the healthier balance sheet, I am reiterating my contrarian Buy rating for the BMY stock.

Caveats To Buy Rating

BMY Technical Indicators

For now, my reiterated BMY Buy rating comes with the caveat that interested investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer before adding.

My caution is attributed to the potential risks arising from the outsized recovery from the recent bottom and the consequently overbought technical indicators across the elevated RSIs and trading volumes, with it remaining to be seen how long the upward momentum may last.

Assuming that BMY is unable to break out of the ongoing retest of the $54s resistance levels, I am of the opinion that the stock is likely to return part of its recent gains to bounce off the $50s support levels.

Given the potential -7.7% correction from current levels, I urge some patience for a lower dollar cost average and an expanded dividend yield/upside potential.

data.title: ETF Year In Review, 2025 Edition
data.description:
By David Mann, Head of Global Exchange-Traded Funds Product and Capital Markets, Franklin Templeton

The "active" in active ETFs simply means there is not an underlying index. This somewhat obvious statement, made last February in my predictions column, was intended to dispel the misconception that active somehow means more risk or more tracking error, which certainly is not the case. In my predictions, I was optimistic that investors would appreciate that nuance and continue to seek active managers to get specific outcomes.

Here is a recap of my 2025 predictions:

  • Active ETF assets under management (AUM) will hit $1.5 trillion in the United States this year.

Wow! I nailed this one. As of December 18, active ETF AUM sits at $1.49 trillion.1 With a few more days left in the year, I might hit this one on the head.

  • There will be more active ETFs than index ETFs in the United States.

Bingo on this one as well. Although I did not expect that out of the 1,011 ETF launches in the United States in 2025, 870 (86%) would be active. Astonishing! The number of active ETFs is now more than 500 ahead of index ETFs - 2,682 to 2,174.2

  • Active fixed-income ETFs will reach 50% of all active net inflows.

I was a little too bullish about the increased adoption of active fixed-income ETFs. Currently, year-to-date net inflows into all active ETFs sit at around $460 billion, representing 31% of all inflow activity. Fixed-income is almost exactly 33% of those active inflows.3

As I was parsing through all the ETF AUM and flow data for 2025 to find any unexpected or surprising trends, it dawned on me that whatever point I wanted to make could be found somewhere in the data. That is what happens when investors are on pace to add over $1.5 trillion of new money into US-listed ETFs, besting last year’s record by over $300 billion.4 US ETF AUM is now almost $13.5 trillion!5

Almost $500 billion into active strategies means there is $1 trillion into index funds. Smart beta might be out of favor but will likely still end up with almost $100 billion of net inflows for the year.6 Heck, when I look at the top four asset gatherers at Franklin Templeton this year, the first is active equity, the second is smart beta, the third is passive market-cap index and the fourth is active fixed-income. Many investors apparently love ETFs, and there really is something for everyone. That is not going to change anytime soon, in my opinion.

Nearly a decade ago, when I began writing, my intent was to identify misconceptions within the ETF arena in case investors were hesitant to choose an ETF because its AUM was too small, its spreads too wide or its trading volume too low. We occasionally continue to have those conversations, but certainly not to the degree that we did in 2015 when US net inflows for the year were at $240 billion and industry AUM was $2.1T.7

That means this column will continue to evolve in 2026. I am debating fewer written articles, while potentially adding videos and/or podcasts. Rest assured that as the ETF industry continues to grow and evolve, I will be here to comment accordingly!

I hope everyone has a wonderful new year and that I’ll see you in 2026!

__________

Endnotes

  1. Source: Bloomberg. As of December 18, 2025.
  2. Ibid.
  3. Ibid.
  4. Ibid.
  5. Ibid.
  6. Source: Morningstar. As of December 18, 2025.
  7. Source: Bloomberg. As of December 18, 2025.

What Are The Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. Performance of a passive portfolio may vary significantly from the performance of an index, as a result of transaction costs, expenses and other factors.

ETFs trade like stocks, fluctuate in market value and may trade above or below the ETF’s net asset value. Brokerage commissions and ETF expenses will reduce returns. ETF shares may be bought or sold throughout the day at their market price on the exchange on which they are listed. However, there can be no guarantee that an active trading market for ETF shares will be developed or maintained or that their listing will continue or remain unchanged. While the shares of ETFs are tradable on secondary markets, they may not readily trade in all market conditions and may trade at significant discounts in periods of market stress.

For actively managed ETFs, there is no guarantee that the manager’s investment decisions will produce the desired results.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TTM Technologies: A Critical AI Hardware Supplier With Undervalued Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

TTM Technologies (NASDAQ: TTMI) is an under the radar winner of the AI hardware boom and rising defense outlays. As of now, demand for high performance tech infrastructure is increasing. And this is because of the AI infrastructure boom. TTMI is an important piece in this boom because it builds the circuit boards and RF components that make it possible. Despite an increase of 189% YTD, I believe TTMI remains undervalued relative to its growth. The company's sales jumped 22% y/y last quarter to $752.7 Mn with record earnings. And honestly, that strength is no fluke. It is driven by secular tailwinds in AI data centers and aerospace markets. Yet, this is exactly where TTMI is deeply specialized. In my view, the stock's valuation hasn't caught up to its new reality.

I've pounded the table this year on AI hardware plays like CommScope (COMM), Seagate (STX), Pagaya Technologies (PGY), Oracle (ORCL) and Western Digital (WDC). These are companies supplying the picks and shovels of the AI revolution. TTM Technologies is cut from the same cloth. Yet it trades at a discount to the hype. This piece lays out why I'm emphatically bullish on TTMI and why I am rating it a Strong Buy. The company has strong multi-end market growth, improving profitability, clear catalysts ahead and a valuation that doesn't fully price its upside. In short, TTMI is a critical enabler in the AI supply chain with the right sector exposure and the wrong price.

Business Overview

TTM Technologies isn't a household name. But it's everywhere inside modern electronics. The company is a leading manufacturer of advanced printed circuit boards and integrated electronic assemblies. It serves customers in mission critical sectors. And maybe the best way I would define TTMI is to think of TTMI as the backbone builder. And this is because it provides complex circuitry and RF components that go into high end hardware. From Aerospace & defense systems to data center and networking gear to medical and industrial equipment's. If Nvidia's (NVDA) cutting edge AI GPUs are the brains, then it means TTMI's ultra high density circuit boards are the nervous system linking them together. And if a fighter jet's radar is its eyes, TTMI likely made the electronic eyelids that house and connect those sensitive sensors.

TTMI is diversified across five end markets. As of Q3 2025, about 45% of sales came from Aerospace & defense. 30% came from data center computing and networking combined. Then 14% came from medical and industrial instrumentation. And finally, 11% came from automotive. This mis is by design. TTMI has steadily moved upmarket into complex, reliability-critical products across sectors where performance and trust matter. For example, TTMI now delivers not just PCBs but entire modules combining sensors, RF and photonics. These target customers who demand top notch quality. This strategic positioning means TTMI isn't slogging in low margin consumer electronics. In fact, it's ingrained in sectors with high barriers to entry and robust demand.

It is important to mention that 80% of TTMI's Q3 sales were tied to two booming arenas. These are defense and AI driven tech. Management explicitly highlighted that three of its five markets (data center, networking and parts of industrial) are seeing growth directly fueled by AI's growing requirements. In fact, nearly all data center and networking revenue is Gen AI related these days. Meanwhile, the Aerospace & Defense side benefits from global security trends. TTMI's role in these sectors is mission critical. When a cloud operator builds a new supercluster, TTMI's high density boards and backplane assemblies are needed to handle the massive data flows. When defense contractors ramp production of next gen missiles or radar systems, TTMI provides complex RF modules and boards those systems rely on.

When I look at the competitive moat, TTMI's moat comes from technology and trust. It has the engineering expertise to make ultra high density interconnect [HDI] PCBs and RF assemblies that few others can match. This is plus long standing relationships with blue chip defense and networking customers.

Growth Drivers

So why am I optimistic that TTMI's run is far from over? Because the macro level catalysts powering TTMI's business are just getting started. And TTMI is exceptionally well positioned to capitalize. First, the surge in gen AI has kicked off an arms race in data center investment. Hyperscale cloud companies, chipmakers and even emerging markets are pouring capital into AI training clusters and high speed networks. Here I am talking GPU farms, optical interconnects, advanced switches. All of which require cutting edge PCB and electronic solution. TTMI has already felt this in 2025 with data center revenues increasing by 44% y/y. I note that management said that " nearly all" of its data center and networking sales are tied to AI driven demand. That trend is not a one quarter fad. It is likely a multi year cycle as AI adoption spreads globally. Each new AI accelerator chassis or high bandwidth router is built on a foundation of PCBs. And this is where TTMI shines best. The company is one of the only few that can reliably produce the ultra complex boards needed for AI workloads. I believe as gen AI matures, TTMI stands to see continued robust orders. Management expects the data center computing segment to keep growing so fast that it will rise from 23% of sales in Q3 to about 28% of sales in Q4. And at the end of the day, the AI gold rush is a rising tide for TTMI, and we're nowhere near its peak.

Secondly, there is the defense spending upside. On the other side of TTMI's business, aerospace and defense demand provides a rock solid backbone. Geopolitical tensions are elevated worldwide [ for example conflicts in Eastern Europe, Middle East and even heightened counterterror needs in Africa]. This drives many countries to boost defense budgets. The US and allies are investing in next gen defense tech, and this is exactly where TTMI is a supplier. Last Q3 20% jump in A&D sales and record backlog reflect this trend. Programs like the AMRAAM missile, advanced surveillance systems, radar for military aircraft, were all cited as contributing TTMI's strong bookings. TTMI's $1.46Bn program backlog will convert to revenue steadily providing long runway of growth.

Last is capacity expansion. A Key part of the TTMI story is that it's investing ahead of demand. This is a classic signoff an opportunity rich outlook. TTMI is currently ramping a new PCB fabrication plant in Penang, Malaysia and building another in Syracuse, New York. These will be focused on ultra high density interconnect tech. These expansions are catalysts in two ways. First, they enable TTMI to capture more revenue. Penang adds capacity to serve the APAC region while Syracuse will give TTMI a state of the art domestic facility to serve high end defense and AI customers who prefer US manufacturing. In Q3, Penang delivered a modest $5Mn in sales as initial production started. But customer interest is quite strong and TTMI is confident that they have announced plans for a second penang facility in the future. This suggest significant pent-up demand once the first plant hits its stride. The CEO highlighted that;

...Penang remains a key part of our China Plus one strategy... We're making very good progress there...

Yet, yields keep improving with each quarter.

Valuation

At about $71 per share as of writing this, TTMI trades approximately at 56X trailing P/E. Well, this sounds high at a first blush. On a TTM EV/EBITDA basis it is about 20x. Bu comparison, legacy electronic manufacturing peers trade at much lower multiples. For instance, Jabil (JBL) is about 12x EV/EBITDA and 36x trailing P/E. On the other side Flex (FLEX) is about 8x EV/EBITDA and 28X P/E. Sanmina (SANM) stands at 11x EV/EBITDA with 34x TTM P/E.

So ideally, yes TTMI carries a premium compared to the broader tech hardware sector. However, that premium is absolutely justified by TTMI's far superior growth. Those peers mostly grow in single digits while TTMI is Printing about 18% top line growth with secular tailwinds at its back. If I look at the FWD earnings, the picture sharpens. TTMI's FWD Non-GAAP P/E is at 29x since earnings are scaling rapidly.

A better gauge is the PEG ratio. When I look at the TTM GAAP PEG ratio, I note that the company is trading at 0.62x while the sector median stands at 1.01x. This implies that the company is trading at a 38% discount to the sector median. Additionally, when I look at the FWD Price/sales, the company is also trading at a 25% discount relative to the sector. When TTMI is trading at 2.54x, the sector median stands at 3.40x as shown below. In conclusion, I would say that TTMI's valuation is not out of line when growth is factored in.

TTMI's Key Risk

The biggest risk I see for TTMI is the AI demand fluctuation. TTMI's recent growth is tied heavily to surging AI hardware orders and strong defense spending. This means that if either of those falter, TTMI's growth could slow. For instance, there is a scenario where the current AI infrastructure spree moderates. Perhaps data center builders digest capacity or a disruptive new technology changes hardware needs. If Gen AI hype cools or tech capex tightens, TTMI's data center and networking segments might see order push out. Similarly, A&D while more stable is not recession proof. Of course, budget priorities can shift with political winds. Any global deescalation in tensions or government austerity moves in defense could soften that 45% of TTMI's business. I believe TTMI is riding two big waves, and waves can ebb. A general economic downturn could also impact automotive and industrial demand for TTMI.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, TTM Technologies is a critical player in massive growth trends, underestimated by the market and backed by tangible results. This year TTMI has proven its mettle. Delivering record revenues and earnings, rising powerful secular waves in AI and defense and investing boldly for the future. Yet even after 189% increase, the stock still trades as if investors aren't fully convinced this story has legs. And that's why I firmly believe it does and reason why I am initiating coverage of TTMI with a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: FS KKR Capital: The Discount Is Not Unjustified, But The Bottom Is In
data.description:
I'm partially on the same page as bears on FS KKR Capital's (FSK) discount to net asset value ("NAV") being justified on the basis of its elevated nonaccruals at fair value and increasingly weaker dividend coverage. However, the BDC grew NAV on a per share basis sequentially during its fiscal 2025 third quarter and recorded net investment income ("NII") that provided robust positive coverage for its truncated aggregate dividend distribution. FSK last paid a quarterly base cash dividend of $0.64 per share, with a supplemental dividend of $0.06 for an aggregate distribution of $0.70 per share. Critically, FSK's dividend distributions from 2026 will see the base reduced by 29.7% to $0.45 per share, and the supplemental payments move to a more variable schedule as per NII. FSK anticipates its aggregate dividend distribution for the first quarter of 2026 will be $0.55 per share.

This would place the company's forward dividend yield at 15%, markedly higher than its large-cap BDC peers. The supplemental component might come in lower in subsequent quarters to compress this yield, but the base means FSK shareholders should, at a minimum, expect to receive a 12.3% dividend yield next year against the BDC's current stock price. Is the yield a buy? It's hard to say yes. While FSK's forward yield would be lower than its trailing twelve month yield of 17.55%, it's still considerably higher than its large-cap peers to reflect the BDC's higher risk profile.

FSK's core metrics are all trending in the wrong direction, raising the specter of another dividend cut and ramping up the risk of NAV per share resuming its dip. Total investment income fell by 15.4% year-over-year to $373 million, as the BDC's weighted average annual yield on accruing debt investments fell by 100 basis points over its year-ago comp. This was in tandem with a deterioration in credit quality that saw nonaccruals at fair value come in at 2.9%. FSK's total investments at fair value also fell to $13.4 billion, a $500 million drop from $13.9 billion a year ago. I last covered the BDC in September, with the Fed cutting rates at back-to-back rate-setting meetings since then. The CME FedWatch Tool has 50 basis points of rate cuts through next year to pull the Fed funds rate lower to 3.00% to 3.25% as its current highest probability outcome at 30.6%.

Credit Quality, NAV Per Share, And Originations

FSK's nonaccruals at fair value dipped from 3% in the second quarter, but was still up by at least 30 basis points from 2.1% in the first quarter. The BDC has now joined the first salvo of BDC dividend cuts, with Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD) also recently reducing its base cash dividend by 33%. FSK's core metrics are not great, with NII per share down from $0.77 a year ago to $0.57 in the third quarter. This represents a decline of 26% with NII also dipping by $0.05 per share sequentially. This means the BDC is covering its reset base dividend by 127%, a roughly 79% payout ratio. Coverage for the aggregate dividend yield next year stands at 104%.

FSK held 58% of its portfolio in first lien senior secured loans, with its second largest allocation to asset based finance. This does ramp up the BDC's risk profile compared to BDCs like Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), which is nearly 100% invested in first lien senior secured loans. This has been a factor driving the divergence in the inherent dividend yield of FSK versus its peers. The BDC needs to ramp up originations to stem the dip in its portfolio and create the conditions for NII growth against the 75 basis points of rate cuts in 2025. FSK's portfolio was 88% invested in floating rate loans at the end of the third quarter.

The BDC's adjusted net investment activity came in at $109 million in the third quarter, a dip from $185 million a year ago and falling sequentially by 65% from $311 million. This happened as its third-quarter NAV hit $6.16 billion, around $21.99 per share. The common shares at $14.59 per share are trading at a material 34% discount to this figure. While NAV grew by $0.06 sequentially, it's been under sustained pressure since 2023, with the move to reset the dividend to a lower level likely set to act as a backstop against further NAV erosion. FSK should save at least $0.19 per share from its lowered base distribution every quarter, a figure that will be reflected in healthier NAV per share numbers in 2026.

Conclusion

FSK's move to reduce its base dividend has placed the ticker on what could be an expanding list of BDCs to cut their dividend in the shadow of recent rate cuts and the specter of further rate cuts. However, the ticker is down 33% year-to-date, and the dividend reduction should help offset what's been a sustained NAV erosion. Hence, NAV could see some recovery next year if originations grow and the variable component of the dividend is not too aggressive. I am rating FSK as a hold.

data.title: 5 Quiet Signals That Lurk In The 2026 Market Outlook
data.description:
For 2026 investing, investors have the S&P 500 (IVV) and its 11 SPDR sub-sectors to consider. The latter choices, which I rated for the new year, are especially compelling. After the S&P 500 index added Carvana (CVNA), insiders are already disposing of shares. The broad ETF leaves retailers holding the stock at a potential peak. Beyond that, investors want to monitor the market outlook for 2026.

The usual risks that investors are accustomed to worrying about in 2025 will persist in 2026, but with a twist. For example, inflation reportedly fell in November, where economists cried foul on the government data, assuming zero housing inflation.

What are the five quiet signals that might emerge in the new year, and what impact will they have on stock markets?

1) Inflation and Yields

On December 18, 2025, the Bureau of Labor Statistics posted that the Consumer Price Index for November increased by 0.2%. It rose by 2.7% in the last 12 months. This set up a stock market uptrend in the Nasdaq (QQQ), Russell 2000 (IWM), and Dow Jones (DJI). The table below shows CPI figures for only three line items.

Gasoline (all times) rose by 3.0%, reversing the -2.1% figure posted the month before. Looking at WTI crude prices in the last month from oilprice.com, the rebound might continue in the near-term.

Gasoline energy inflation is a potential risk that might raise CPI figures. That would weaken the Federal Reserve’s ability to cut interest rates again by 25 bps.

2) Japanese Bond Yields

The rise in Japanese bond yields takes precedence over that observed in the U.S. Treasury market. The former is experiencing yields not seen in decades. On Monday, Japan's 10-year government bond yields rose to 2.100%. This is the highest level seen since February 1999. Yields rose in response to the Bank of Japan continuing to raise interest rates. In early December, 30-year Japanese bonds reached 3.445%.

The yields firmly ended the Abenomics world. Started in 2012/13, Abenomics had “three arrows” in its policy. It expanded monetary policy, endorsed fiscal flexibility by delaying consumption tax increases, and implemented structural reforms to liberalize trade.

Today, Japan’s trade tensions with the U.S. further define post-Abenomics. Its deal with the U.S. includes a duty rate of 15%. Fortunately, tariffs did not hurt exports in November. Japan logged a 322.3 billion yen (US$2 billion) trade surplus that month.

Japan’s rising yields will affect U.S. stock and bond markets. It increases the odds of an abrupt, de-risking episode. If Japan's bond yields spike too quickly, it raises the attractiveness of the Yen. The U.S. dollar might weaken, causing U.S. Treasury yields to rise to attract foreign investors.

For now, the U.S. Dollar Index (UUP) (DXY) traded in a range since the summer. However, DXY is down by over 9% in the last year.

A persistently weak currency would increase the cost of imports for American firms. Firms might pass the higher costs to consumers. Stocks like Walmart (WMT) and Dollar Tree (DLTR) dipped recently. Dollar General (DG) traded near its 52-week high, while Costco (COST) traded near a 52-week low.

In the scorecard above, Walmart has the highest industry rank. It scores an A- or higher on EPS revisions, momentum, and profitability. All of the retailers trade at a premium valuation.

3) AI Bubble Gets Bigger

Technology stocks briefly sold off after Oracle’s (ORCL) CDS prices rose. Markets grew increasingly concerned that the software giant’s debt safety profile worsened. Markets inferred from Microsoft’s (MSFT) results that OpenAI’s quarterly expenses topped $12 billion. With competition for AI services mounting from Alphabet’s (GOOGL) Gemini Pro, OpenAI is not likely to reach break-even levels in 2026.

Concerns dissipated as fast as they started: Micron’s pivot out of the consumer business reminded markets that demand for memory chips used in AI servers is healthy.

The AI bubble concern is not a quiet signal, since the media provides coverage of it regularly. The strong pricing power for memory throughout 2026-2027 suggests that the AI bubble, measured by price-to-earnings valuations, will expand. Investors should envision the “K” economy widening.

Excessive capital expenditures in anything AI-related will create shortages in other sectors. For example, the PC builders like Dell Technologies (DELL), HP Inc. (HPQ), and Lenovo (LNVGY) will need to pay 100% or more for the memory components. The AI server market may cause technology components in vehicles to rise. That would hurt Aptiv (APTV) and STMicroelectronics (STM).

4) Bitcoin Rebounds

Strategy (MSTR) recently decided to pause Bitcoin (BTC-USD) (BTC) purchases. It opted to boost its cash reserve to around $2.2 billion. Investors skeptical about cryptocurrencies might assume that BTC prices fall below Strategy’s average price paid of $74,972. However, Bitcoin has a history of rebounding when markets are most pessimistic.

Ethereum (ETH) might not rebound as quickly. The assets under management for the ETH ETF are $2.13 billion, compared to $4.31 billion for the BTC ETF. This suggests that investors prefer Bitcoin over Ethereum and other cryptocurrencies.

5) Federal Reserve Chair Transition

Politically based expectations are the least predictable. However, the leadership transition of the Federal Reserve Chair is in 2026. The U.S. President will announce a successor to Fed Chair Powell. Powell’s term ends in May 2026. In the last Fed question and answer period, Powell did not say if he would stay on as a member of the Board.

A quiet signal that markets might ignore is the Federal Reserve’s independence. A newly appointed Fed Chair who only responds to the stock market would end up undermining it. The economy needs a Federal Reserve that strictly has a dual mandate of maximum employment and price stability.

Your Takeaway

Stock markets typically do not price in quiet signals that lurk in the background. When they emerge, the market mechanism adjusts stock prices to account for their risks. Investors should consider the five signals that might undermine the stock market’s strong performance in 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EWP: Spanish Stocks To Benefit From Solid GDP Growth In 2026
data.description:
Introduction

Spain remains one of the fastest-growing European economies, with GDP growth seen at 2.9% in 2025 before moderating to 2.3% in 2026. This has naturally translated into strong returns for Spain-focused ETFs, with the iShares MSCI Spain ETF (EWP) delivering a ~78% gain, far above the ~19% total return achieved by the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO):

Aside from strong economic momentum, a ~14% gain in the EUR/USD exchange rate has also contributed to EWP's strong returns measured in USD terms.

Looking ahead to 2026, I confirm my previous Buy rating on EWP, driven by:

  • A trailing P/E of 14.82x, offering a wide discount to the S&P 500 even if we account for weaker expected earnings growth.
  • A well-covered dividend of 2.28%, making EWP an attractive ETF for income-focused investors amid Fed rate cuts.
  • Expectations for a small boost to returns in USD terms from a stronger EUR.

In this article, I will expand on these points and discuss risks related to the investment case.

ETF Overview

You can access all EWP information on the iShares website here. The iShares MSCI Spain ETF invests in large-cap Spanish stocks (the benchmark index is the MSCI Spain 25/50 Index). The ETF's portfolio is very concentrated, holding just 22 companies. As such, the exposure to the top ten holdings stands at ~75.06%, roughly double the concentration observed in the S&P 500:

EWP's holdings are primarily allocated to cyclical sectors such as financials (44.02% of ETF assets), industrials (13.57%) and consumer discretionary (13.57%), only partially offset by a sizable 21.74% allocation to the defensive utilities sector:

Given the overweight position in cyclical sectors, EWP tends to do well when the Spanish economy is exhibiting robust GDP growth, which is the outlook over the near term.

EWP's expense ratio stands at 0.5%, in line with other country-specific ETFs, but above the 0.1-0.2% expense ratio I generally recommend for long-term passive ETF holdings. Given the outsized allocation to just a few Spanish large-cap stocks, one can reasonably consider investing in EWP's largest positions directly, thus saving on ETF fees while achieving a similar (but not identical) performance compared to EWP.

Alternatively, you can view EWP as a tactical holding to gain exposure to the attractive valuations offered by Spanish stocks, which I will discuss below.

Current valuation

In light of the outsized 44.02% allocation to the financials sector (which traditionally trades at a discount to the overall market), it is no surprise that EWP offers one of the lowest valuations you can find for a country-specific ETF. Indeed EWP's trailing P/E stands at just 14.82x (earnings yield of 6.75%), a circa 48% discount to the 28.38x trailing P/E for the S&P 500.

From an earnings growth perspective, I expect EWP's earnings to grow in the low-to-mid single digits in 2026 as financial companies grapple with lower interest rates in the Eurozone (the ECB delivered 1% in rate cuts in H1 2025). This will be notably weaker than the circa 14% earnings growth for the S&P 500, which will benefit from a projected 2.3% increase in U.S. GDP next year, as well as higher earnings stemming from overseas sales thanks to a weaker U.S. dollar.

Even if we account for EWP's weaker projected earnings growth however, from a valuation standpoint, I believe EWP is the clear winner, at least as long as Spanish GDP keeping growing at a robust pace. I expect earnings growth for EWP to pick up in 2027, benefitting from a stabilization in ECB interest rates, while S&P 500 earnings growth will likely moderate as GDP growth slows down to 2% in 2027.

EUR/USD outlook

Aside from attractive current valuations, the second key driver for EWP in 2026 will the changes in the EUR/USD exchange rate.

I see the EUR/USD pair driven principally by potential progress on a ceasefire in the Ukraine/Russia war. If we do get a ceasefire, the EUR will likely gain in value as energy prices remain low. Alternatively, an escalation in fighting, associated with higher energy prices, will likely result in a stronger USD.

The ECB's December 2025 macroeconomic projections show inflation at or below the 2% inflation target in 2026-2028, indicating little need to move rates up or down over the near term. As such, focus will turn to the Federal Reserve, which will likely continue with its policy normalization, with the central bank projecting one 0.25% rate cut, while markets price in two 0.25% cuts.

The net result, as priced in by EUR/USD futures, should be a small gain for the EUR/USD exchange rate to about 1.20 at the end of 2026:

As such, EWP returns in USD terms should get a small boost from a weaker USD in 2026, all else equal.

EWP Rating

Spanish stocks are clearly not as attractive as when I last covered EWP back in July 2025. Back then the trailing P/E stood at 11.65x versus 14.82x currently. Still, with the S&P 500 trading at all-time highs, I see further incremental outperformance for EWP in 2026, with the robust earnings growth for the S&P 500 arguably priced in given the elevated 28.38x multiple.

Amid ongoing Fed rate cuts, EWP also remains interesting for income-focused investors as it offers a 2.28% dividend yield (payout ratio of 0.41x if we account for the 0.5% in ETF expenses), although the outsized allocation in financial stocks has clearly resulted in inconsistent dividend growth, surging 14.39% over the past five years but standing at only 1.18% over the past decade:

All in all, taking into account a small expected boost from a weaker USD and the 0.5% expense ratio, I see EWP trading at about $56.50/share at the end of 2026, implying a mid-to-high single digit total return next year. This should be enough for the ETF to outperform the S&P 500, confirming a Buy rating on EWP.

As discussed in the ETF Overview section, investors may potentially boost returns by investing directly in EWP's largest components, thus saving on the 0.5% expense fee.

Risks

Given the overweight position in cyclical sectors, a downside surprise in Spanish GDP growth will likely weigh on EWP's performance relative to the S&P 500. One way to hedge the high allocation to financials in particular is to invest in European REITs which should outperform should the ECB resume rate cuts next year (which in my opinion is possible but not the base case as things stand).

EWP also has no exposure to the information technology sector, which could be a blessing or a curse depending on the performance of the AI trade next year. I see the glass half empty with regards to the AI trade, hence I view the nonexistent information technology sector as more of a plus. Still, if you insist on having exposure to the AI trade, you may want to consider complementing your EWP allocation with individual holdings of European IT companies.

I should also note that a reversal of recent EUR strenght will be detrimenal to returns in USD terms as EWP's value stems from the local prices of Spanish stocks traded in EUR.

Last but not least, I should note that while Spanish GDP growth remains robust, it is clearly decelerating, with 2027 GDP expansion seen at 2%. In essence, Spain is currently benefiting from a one-off reduction of the unemployment rate which remains elevated at about 10.4% in 2025. Once the unemployment rate stops falling, GDP expansion will likely moderate as well:

The good news is that public finances remain robust, with a budget deficit of 2.5% in 2025 and a projected decline in the debt/GDP ratio to 97.1% by 2027.

Conclusion

Spanish stocks have had a stellar performance in 2025, benefitting from low starting valuations, a more attractive earnings outlook for financials in light of a pause in ECB rate cuts, with returns in USD terms boosted by a stronger Euro.

Looking ahead 2026, I expect returns to moderate, with EWP delivering a total return in the mid-to-high single digits, principally driven by strong performance cyclical stocks and a small boost from a weaker USD.

I confirm my Buy rating on EWP, notwithstanding a drag on returns from an elevated expense ratio as well as risks stemming from the ETF's outsized allocation to cyclical sectors which may backfire if GDP growth surprises to the downside.

Thank you for reading.
